chunk_summary: Guidelines for early post-gastrostomy feeding in children with neurodisability, focusing on feeding initiation, infection prevention, and emergency responses to complications like tube dislodgement or peritonitis.
learning_objectives: Understand the protocol for initiating feeding post-gastrostomy in stable pediatric patients.
Recognize signs of peristomal infection and appropriate first-response actions.
Learn emergency management of early tube dislodgement and associated risks.
Identify factors contributing to post-placement vomiting and appropriate interventions.
key_facts: Feeding can begin within hours post-gastrostomy in stable children using iso-osmolar formula, starting with conservative volumes.
Peristomal infection risk is reduced with single-dose peri-procedure antibiotic prophylaxis aligned with local antimicrobial policy.
Accidental tube dislodgement within the first 6–12 weeks is an emergency; blind replacement should be avoided.
Persistent pneumoperitoneum beyond 3 days requires exclusion of bowel injury.
Bolus feeding is preferred unless contraindicated by severe reflux, retching, or intolerance.
reference_quote: Accidental tube dislodgement in the early period is a true emergency because the tract is immature (often considered <6–12 weeks).
